title: Using feedback

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Using feedback; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Organizing Instruction and Study to Improve Student Learning; 2007; section applicable to Using feedback
sources.claim: Useful feedback is converted into a diagnosis, an explained correction, a new exposing test, and an independent retry rather than copied as a replacement answer.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Useful feedback is converted into a diagnosis, an explained correction, a new exposing test, and an independent retry rather than copied as a replacement answer.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: After an off-by-one answer fails, the learner identifies the invalid last index, corrects the bound, adds empty and one-item tests, and solves a new traversal problem. Your goal is to explain why an answer failed and demonstrate a corrected transfer attempt. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Debugging and testing loop; Reading before writing. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Useful feedback is converted into a diagnosis, an explained correction, a new exposing test, and an independent retry rather than copied as a replacement answer. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Feedback can be incomplete or wrong, scores alone do not identify causes, and a corrected near-copy does not prove transfer to a new context. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Once the correct answer is shown, recognizing it means the underlying skill has been learned.

includes: Convert correctness feedback into a diagnosis, correction and new test.
Explain why an answer failed and demonstrate a corrected transfer attempt.
excludes: Feedback can be incomplete or wrong, scores alone do not identify causes, and a corrected near-copy does not prove transfer to a new context.

outcomes: Explain why an answer failed and demonstrate a corrected transfer attempt.

examples.title: Using feedback: direct case
examples.situation: After an off-by-one answer fails, the learner identifies the invalid last index, corrects the bound, adds empty and one-item tests, and solves a new traversal problem.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: After an off-by-one answer fails, the learner identifies the invalid last index, corrects the bound, adds empty and one-item tests, and solves a new traversal problem.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Explain the failure in your own words, produce evidence that the correction works, and complete a delayed or surface-different attempt without the shown answer.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Explain the failure in your own words, produce evidence that the correction works, and complete a delayed or surface-different attempt without the shown answer.

examples.title: Using feedback: boundary case
examples.situation: A vague comment about clarity is translated into one specific naming or decomposition change and verified against the original behavior.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A vague comment about clarity is translated into one specific naming or decomposition change and verified against the original behavior.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Feedback can be incomplete or wrong, scores alone do not identify causes, and a corrected near-copy does not prove transfer to a new context.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Once the correct answer is shown, recognizing it means the underlying skill has been learned.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Explain why an answer failed and demonstrate a corrected transfer attempt.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Once the correct answer is shown, recognizing it means the underlying skill has been learned.
misconceptions.correction: Explain the failure in your own words, produce evidence that the correction works, and complete a delayed or surface-different attempt without the shown answer.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Once the correct answer is shown, recognizing it means the underlying skill has been learned.

analogy.example: Sports coaching can model Using feedback: study a play, attempt it, use specific feedback, retry independently, and credit the people or material that helped.
usefulFor: Connecting explanation, feedback, independent transfer, assistance, and integrity
limitations: Learning evidence and reuse permissions are governed by explicit course and software rules, not only informal coaching norms.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to explain why an answer failed and demonstrate a corrected transfer attempt. Keep this boundary in view: Feedback can be incomplete or wrong, scores alone do not identify causes, and a corrected near-copy does not prove transfer to a new context.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Using feedback without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Once the correct answer is shown, recognizing it means the underlying skill has been learned.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A vague comment about clarity is translated into one specific naming or decomposition change and verified against the original behavior.
